text: The surgeon makes an incision at the umbilicus and inserts a trocar, followed by placing a camera through the trocar to visualize the abdominal cavity. The abdomen is inflated with carbon dioxide, and the skin is numbed for additional incisions. Three more trocars are inserted through the abdominal wall, providing access for surgical instruments.

The surgeon retracts the gallbladder upward, revealing gallstones inside. Using gentle dissection, the surgeon teases away fat near the cystic duct, progressively exposing the duct that connects the gallbladder to the common bile duct.
The surgeon identifies the pulsatile cystic artery behind the cystic duct, which supplies blood to the gallbladder, as it comes into view.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery and cystic duct, ensuring secure closure before dividing these structures to prevent bleeding or bile leakage. The cystic duct is divided first, followed by the cystic artery.

The surgeon uses an electric cautery device to dissect the gallbladder from the liver, with visible smoke generated by the cautery process. The device effectively cauterizes bleeding points during the dissection.
The surgeon continues to dissect the gallbladder from the liver using an electrocautery device, progressively freeing the gallbladder from the liver bed until it is completely detached.
The surgeon places the detached gallbladder into a retrieval bag for removal from the body, following its dissection from the liver.

The surgeon inspects the area under the liver, confirming that the cystic duct and cystic artery are securely clipped with no leakage or bleeding. The liver surface is also checked for any signs of bleeding.
The surgeon pulls the gallbladder out of the body through the upper incision by retrieving the string attached to the bag containing the gallbladder.
The surgeon cuts open the gallbladder, revealing golden stones inside. One stone is approximately one centimeter in size.
